title: When Did the Drift Start, Quietly
subtitle: A journal prompt set for tracing the slow drift in a long partnership
excerpt: A twenty-minute journal prompt set for the long partnership where something has gone quiet. The drift was small once. The prompts trace where it started and what it has cost.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The trace-the-drift shape is common in writing on long-term partnership and on relationship therapy.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the partnership has gone quiet and you cannot quite say when it started. Sit with the prompts on your own first; you may want to share parts of what you wrote later.

content.text: A journal prompt set for the long partnership where something has gone quiet. Six prompts, about twenty minutes. The first two open wide. The last four trace the drift.
New to journal prompts as a practice? Read
journal prompts as practice
first.
The practice
Warm-up
When were we last properly easy with each other?
Write the rough memory. The week, the season, the year. Two or three sentences.
What was happening in our lives then?
Work, money, children, family. The shape of the week. Whatever made up the life around the relationship.
Closer in
What changed first, that I can name?
A move, a job, a baby, a loss, an illness, a money pressure, a quiet shift. Name the first thing that started the drift.
What stopped being a habit, that used to be one?
The Sunday morning together. The shared meal. The walk. The bedtime that used to be a together-time. Name two or three things that quietly went.
What did the drift cost me, on the inside?
Be plain. Loneliness in the room. The feeling of being unseen. The slow change in how I talk to my friends about us. The thing I do not say at family meals.
What is one habit I could try to bring back this month?

One. Not a long list. A walk every Sunday morning. A real meal together at the table once a week. A conversation in the car with the radio off. Pick the smallest one that you would have a real chance of doing.
Where this practice comes from
Original to homemade.education. The trace-the-drift shape is a common one. It appears in writing on long-term partnership and in therapy books.
